claims.claim_text: A named 2025 fintech breach at QuantEdge Solutions cost $12.5 million.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In 2025, a major fintech company, "QuantEdge Solutions," experienced a catastrophic data leak that cost them $12.5 million in regulatory fines and customer compensation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A fragile AI tool integration can turn into a serious data leak and compliance incident when authorization and schema handling are not designed defensively.
claims.rationale: This is a named, quantified incident with no attributable source and should not remain as a precise historical anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The QuantEdge incident was caused by stale prompt-injected JSON schemas, hallucinated fields, and leaked financial records in unauthenticated sessions.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: When they updated the underlying database schema to support new asset classes, they forgot to update the fragile prompt-injected JSON schema the model relied upon. The model hallucinated the missing fields, misinterpreting authorization flags, and subsequently dumped the highly sensitive financial records of thousands of users into the chat interface of unauthenticated sessions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When tool integrations depend on stale prompt-defined schemas instead of validated interfaces, schema drift can lead to incorrect field handling and dangerous authorization failures.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is valid, but the specific failure chain is presented as a real incident without a source.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic publicly introduced MCP in November 2024 as an open standard for connecting AI assistants to external systems and data.
claims.span_hint: sections: Why This Module Matters; Did You Know
claims.rationale: Anthropic's launch post directly dates and describes the public introduction of MCP.

claims.claim_text: MCP initialization begins with `initialize`, followed by `notifications/initialized`, and includes capability negotiation and protocol version agreement.
claims.span_hint: sections: Architectural Deep Dive; Protocol Internals
claims.rationale: The lifecycle spec explicitly defines the initialization sequence and negotiated fields.

claims.claim_text: MCP resources are URI-identified server-exposed data used to provide readable context to clients.
claims.span_hint: section: Capability Primitives, Resources bullet
claims.rationale: The resources spec directly defines resources, URIs, and read operations.

claims.claim_text: MCP tools are callable actions with structured arguments, and clients discover and invoke them via `tools/list` and `tools/call`.
claims.span_hint: sections: Capability Primitives, Tools bullet; Consuming MCP
claims.rationale: The tools spec names the discovery and invocation methods and describes tool schemas.

claims.claim_text: MCP prompts are reusable prompt templates exposed by servers and intended to be user-controlled.
claims.span_hint: section: Capability Primitives, Prompts bullet
claims.rationale: The prompts spec directly states both the primitive and its user-controlled interaction model.

claims.claim_text: MCP defines two standard transports: stdio and Streamable HTTP.
claims.span_hint: section: Transport Mechanisms
claims.rationale: The transport spec explicitly enumerates the standard transport mechanisms.

claims.claim_text: In stdio mode, the client launches the server as a subprocess, messages flow over stdin/stdout, and stdout must contain only valid MCP messages while logging can go to stderr.
claims.span_hint: sections: Transport Mechanisms; Common Mistakes row 1; Quiz 2
claims.rationale: The stdio transport rules are stated verbatim in the official transport spec.

claims.claim_text: Streamable HTTP uses a single MCP endpoint that supports POST and GET, and may use SSE to stream multiple server messages.
claims.span_hint: section: Transport Mechanisms, Streamable HTTP bullet
claims.rationale: The Streamable HTTP transport spec directly describes the endpoint shape and optional SSE behavior.

claims.claim_text: Streamable HTTP servers must validate the `Origin` header to prevent DNS rebinding, and invalid origins are rejected with HTTP 403 in the clarified 2025-11-25 spec.
claims.span_hint: section: Security, Authentication, and Production Deployment, DNS Rebinding Protection bullet
claims.rationale: The spec and changelog explicitly cover Origin validation and the later clarification of HTTP 403 handling.

claims.claim_text: MCP authorization is optional; HTTP-based transports should follow the authorization spec, while stdio implementations should not use that flow and should obtain credentials from the environment.
claims.span_hint: section: Security, Authentication, and Production Deployment, Streamable HTTP intro
claims.rationale: The authorization spec states these transport-specific requirements directly.

claims.claim_text: MCP's HTTP authorization model is based on OAuth 2.1, and MCP servers must implement OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) while clients must use it for authorization-server discovery.
claims.span_hint: section: Security, Authentication, and Production Deployment, OAuth bullet
claims.rationale: The 2025-11-25 authorization spec explicitly names OAuth 2.1 and RFC 9728 requirements.

claims.claim_text: MCP clients must implement PKCE, use `S256` when technically capable, and verify PKCE support from authorization-server metadata before proceeding.
claims.span_hint: section: Security, Authentication, and Production Deployment, OAuth bullet 1
claims.rationale: The authorization spec contains explicit PKCE and `S256` requirements.

claims.claim_text: A named early-2026 community MCP server incident allegedly allowed `/etc/shadow` exfiltration through a `read_file(filepath)` bug.
claims.span_hint: section: The Standard I/O (stdio) Trust Boundary
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: In early 2026, a popular community-built MCP server designed to analyze local git repositories contained a subtle vulnerability. The tool `read_file(filepath)` lacked path canonicalization checks. A malicious prompt injected into an open-source repository instructed the agent to "analyze the configuration file located at `../../../../etc/shadow`". Because the MCP server ran with elevated local privileges and failed to validate the path boundary, the agent successfully read and exfiltrated the shadow password file to an external endpoint via another tool.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A filesystem tool that accepts paths without canonicalization and boundary checks can be tricked into reading files outside its intended directory, including sensitive system files.
claims.rationale: This is a specific security incident with dates, actors, and outcome but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Path-like inputs such as `../../../etc/passwd` can enable arbitrary file reads if a filesystem tool does not canonicalize and constrain paths.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, filesystem canonicalization row
claims.rationale: OWASP documents path traversal and explains how `../` sequences can expose arbitrary files.

claims.claim_text: Logging stray text to stdout in stdio mode will always crash the client parser.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 1
claims.anchor_text: Stray text corrupts the stream and crashes the client parser.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Stray text corrupts the stream and can cause the client to reject or misparse messages.
claims.rationale: The spec supports stream corruption, but the guaranteed parser-crash phrasing overstates the outcome.

claims.claim_text: A 50MB tool result will immediately exhaust the model context window and fail the session.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, massive datasets row
claims.anchor_text: Returning a 50MB database dump as a tool result will overwhelm the LLM's context window, leading to immediate token limit exhaustion and session failure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Returning a very large dataset as a tool result can overwhelm the model's context window or exceed request limits.
claims.rationale: The precise payload size and guaranteed failure mode are illustrative, not sourced protocol facts.

claims.claim_text: MCP was introduced to unify over 50 bespoke tool-calling implementations.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: aiming to unify a fragmented ecosystem of over 50 different bespoke tool-calling implementations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: to reduce fragmentation across many bespoke tool-calling integrations.
claims.rationale: The exact count is unsourced and should be removed rather than cited loosely.

claims.claim_text: A standard MCP `CallToolRequest` payload is typically under 500 bytes.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: A standard MCP `CallToolRequest` payload is typically less than 500 bytes, making it highly efficient for high-frequency agentic interactions compared to traditional heavy REST payloads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: MCP `tools/call` payloads are lightweight JSON-RPC messages, but their size depends on the tool name and argument payload.
claims.rationale: The byte count and comparative performance claim are too precise without a benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: Enterprise teams have reported a 60 percent reduction in new-model integration time after adopting MCP.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: By adopting MCP, enterprise engineering teams have reported a 60 percent reduction in the time required to integrate new Large Language Models into their existing internal tooling infrastructure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Adopting a shared protocol like MCP can reduce the integration overhead of supporting multiple models and internal tools.
claims.rationale: This quantified business outcome is unsourced and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: JSON-RPC 2.0 was originally specified in 2005.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: The underlying communication structure of MCP relies on JSON-RPC 2.0, a stateless, lightweight remote procedure call protocol that was originally specified all the way back in 2005.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The underlying communication structure of MCP relies on JSON-RPC 2.0.
claims.rationale: The historical date is not backed here and is risky to state precisely without a trusted source in the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Any MCP-compliant client can instantly use a server with no custom configuration.
claims.span_hint: section: The Evolution of Tool Calling and the Genesis of MCP, final paragraph
claims.anchor_text: By adopting MCP, you write a server for your data source *once*, and any MCP-compliant AI client can instantly understand and utilize it without any custom configuration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By adopting MCP, you can expose a data source through one standard protocol so compatible clients can discover and use its capabilities without bespoke per-client tool wiring, though deployment and authorization still need configuration.
claims.rationale: The interoperability point is valid, but "instantly" and "without any custom configuration" overclaim what deployments require.

claims.claim_text: stdio has zero network overhead and is highly secure because it stays within process boundaries.
claims.span_hint: section: Transport Mechanisms, stdio bullet
claims.anchor_text: This has zero network overhead and is highly secure as it remains within the host operating system's process boundaries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This avoids network transport overhead and keeps communication within local process boundaries, but the server still runs with the host user's operating-system permissions.
claims.rationale: The local-process description is supportable, but the absolute security framing is misleading.

further_reading.title: Model Context Protocol specification repository
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the official spec and documentation repository for MCP primitives, lifecycle, transports, and schema.

further_reading.title: Introducing the Model Context Protocol
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the public launch post that explains why MCP was created and what problem it targets.

further_reading.title: RFC 9728: OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata
further_reading.why_relevant: This RFC underpins MCP's authorization-server discovery model for protected HTTP transports.

further_reading.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper provides the primary reference for the ReAct technique named in the module's historical framing.

notes: No shared section pool selected. The two named war stories and all three quantified Did You Know metrics appear unsourced and should be rewritten rather than cited. Supported protocol claims use official upstream MCP materials, OWASP, Anthropic, and RFC 9728.
